channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3091285: Composer scaffolding fails when permissions on default.settings.yml or default.settings.php is not writable.
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +92/−10 · php +92/−10
  • Diff: 0665057
  • Discussion: #3091285 · 52 contributors · 161 comments
  • Followers: 146

Summary

After Drupal's installation process hardens the sites/default directory (setting permissions to read-only), subsequent Composer commands that trigger the scaffold plugin would throw a RuntimeException and abort, sometimes leaving composer.json and composer.lock out of sync. The fix makes the scaffold plugin automatically and temporarily loosen permissions on the destination directory before writing a scaffold file, then restore the original permissions when the operation completes. Sites that added file-mapping workarounds in composer.json to skip scaffolding of default.settings.php and default.services.yml no longer need those workarounds.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The fix is applied to ReplaceOp::process() in composer/Plugin/Scaffold/Operations/ReplaceOp.php. Before removing and replacing a scaffold file, the method now captures the original fileperms() values of both the destination directory and the destination file (if they exist). If the destination directory is not writable, it calls the new protected makeWritable(string $filepath): bool method, which applies chmod($path, ($mod & 0777) | 0200) to add the owner write bit. The actual remove-and-copy (or symlink) operation then runs inside a try/finally block that restores the captured original permissions on both the directory and the file, regardless of whether the scaffold write succeeded or failed. If makeWritable() itself returns false (the running process lacks sufficient OS privileges to change permissions), a RuntimeException is thrown with an interpolated message identifying the path. The symlink path bypasses the post-write file-permission restoration since the symlink inherits source permissions. makeWritable() is added as a protected method on the base class shared with AppendOp, making it available for future use in other operations.

Test infrastructure in FixturesBase::io() gains a bool $reset = FALSE parameter to allow flushing the BufferIO between assertions in a single test. A new test case in ReplaceOpTest verifies that process() succeeds when both the target file and its parent directory have been chmod'd to non-writable, that file content is correctly updated, that the restrictive permissions are preserved after the operation, and that the symlink variant does not alter permissions.

Upgrade

No action required. Sites using the file-mapping workaround in composer.json to skip scaffolding of default.settings.php or default.services.yml may remove those entries if the sole purpose was to avoid the permissions failure:

// These workaround entries are no longer necessary:
"file-mapping": {
    "[web-root]/sites/default/default.services.yml": false,
    "[web-root]/sites/default/default.settings.php": false
}

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 30, 2019
  • Committed: July 9, 2026 (6 years and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • mglaman (Centarro)
  • mile23
  • afeijo
  • Mixologic (Drupal Association)
  • lolandese (Cognizant Technology Solutions)
  • larowlan (PreviousNext)
  • jannakha (Tomato Elephant Studio)
  • mmjvb
  • thursday_bw
  • xmacinfo (xMac info)
  • tedbow (Acquia)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • vakulrai (QED42)
  • emersonreis.dev (Zoocha)
  • trackleft2 (The University of Arizona)
  • nmudgal (Material)
  • greg.1.anderson (Pantheon)
  • Marsell key
  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3607866: Scaffold plugin does not always create autoload_runtime.php when upgrading to 11.4
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +215/−0 · php +199 · json +16
  • Diff: 7f3e251
  • Discussion: #3607866 · 23 contributors · 70 comments
  • Followers: 39

Summary

When running composer update to upgrade from Drupal 11.3.x to 11.4.x, the new autoload_runtime.php stub file was sometimes not written to the web root. Because web/index.php in 11.4 requires that file, the site immediately returned an HTTP 500 error after the update. The workaround was to run composer install a second time after the update completed. This fix eliminates the need for that second invocation.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Drupal 11.4 introduced autoload_runtime.php (a stub that delegates to vendor/autoload_runtime.php for the Symfony Runtime integration), and the drupal/core-composer-scaffold plugin was updated to generate that file via Handler::scaffold(). The problem is a class-loading race inside a single Composer process.

When composer update runs, Composer fires post-package-install/post-package-update events as each package is extracted. If any package that declares plugin-modifies-downloads (or any plugin that triggers postPackage() on the scaffold plugin) is installed before drupal/core-composer-scaffold itself is updated on disk, PHP loads and pins the 11.3 version of Handler for the remainder of the process. Composer does reload Plugin.php itself via eval() after the scaffold package is replaced (see PluginManager::registerPackage()), but Handler was already in PHP's class cache and cannot be reloaded. So when the final postCmd event fires handler()->scaffold(), the stale 11.3 Handler code runs and skips autoload_runtime.php generation entirely.

The fix adds ensureAutoloadRuntimeFile() directly to Plugin, which is always reloaded with the new code. It is called from postCmd() immediately after handler()->scaffold(). The method uses ManageOptions, AllowedPackages, GenerateAutoloadRuntimeReferenceFile, and ManageGitIgnore directly (without going through Handler) and is a no-op when the file already exists or when no allowed packages are configured. A @todo marks it for removal once upgrades from 11.3 are no longer supported.

The regression test introduces a fixture Composer plugin (fixtures/composer-plugin-downloads-modifier) that declares "plugin-modifies-downloads": true, which causes Composer's dependency resolver (Transaction::movePluginsToFront()) to install it ahead of the scaffold plugin update, reliably reproducing the stale-Handler scenario. The test then asserts both that the stale handler's marker string (SCAFFOLD_TEST_STALE_HANDLER) appeared in output and that autoload_runtime.php was nonetheless created by the refreshed Plugin code.

Upgrade

No code changes are required in projects. Sites that already ran into the 500 error can recover by running:

composer install

After this fix ships in drupal/core-composer-scaffold, a single composer update will correctly produce autoload_runtime.php. If your build pipeline copies only tracked Git files, ensure autoload_runtime.php is not listed in your web root's .gitignore (it is added there by the scaffold plugin and must be included in deployment artifacts).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 2, 2026
  • Committed: July 9, 2026 (7 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phily
  • xmacinfo (xMac info)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • kentr
  • godotislate

Collaboration

longwave diagnosed the root cause through direct instrumentation: they added debug_print_backtrace() to the Handler constructor and observed that the constructor was called while the 11.3 plugin code was still on disk, confirming that Handler was locked in PHP's class cache before the scaffold package was overwritten. That specific debugging session (comments #50 and #51) identified the class-loading race and the eval() mechanism Composer uses to reload only the Plugin class, which pointed directly to the solution: put the recovery logic in Plugin rather than Handler.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3609363: Regression: install_import_translations() no longer lets contrib/custom translations override core on install-from-config
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 8 files, +110/−2 · php +93/−1 · yml +17/−1
  • Diff: 7056932
  • Discussion: #3609363 · 5 contributors · 14 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Sites that install from existing configuration with a non-English default language could lose contrib or custom translation overrides after upgrading to 11.4.x. Core translations would silently "win" over any overriding .po file provided by a contrib module (such as locale_deploy) or a custom project. This is now fixed and the 11.3.x behavior is restored.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The 11.4.x locale batch-to-services refactor changed install_import_translations() in core/includes/install.core.inc to call \Drupal::service(LocaleFetch::class)->buildUpdateBatch() with no arguments. That call imports every project's translations in a single "first-wins" pass: PoDatabaseWriter uses empty overwrite_options, meaning the first import of a string can never be overwritten. Projects are processed in getAll() order, so drupal is imported first and its strings are locked in before contrib or custom projects run. The subsequent "last-wins" pass in install_finish_translations() then finds every file already recorded in {locale_file} and re-imports nothing, so custom overrides are permanently lost.

In 11.3.x, install_import_translations() explicitly imported only the drupal project, deferring all contrib and custom projects to install_finish_translations(), which runs with last-wins semantics. The fix restores that contract with a one-character change: buildUpdateBatch(['drupal']). A functional test was also added to MultilingualInstallerTest, covering the install-from-config path with two new hidden test modules (locale_test_additional and locale_test_extra) that supply competing Spanish translations for the same source string; the test asserts that the last-wins override from locale_test_extra is the one that survives.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 8, 2026
  • Committed: July 9, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • chr.fritsch (Thunder)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

alexpott diagnosed the regression by placing the 11.3.x and 11.4.x implementations of install_import_translations() side by side in a comment and immediately identifying the missing ['drupal'] argument. He then spent considerable effort writing a functional test for the fix, noting in a comment that the installer translation code is "convoluted and full of side effects" and that even AI assistance did not speed up the test-writing process. catch, on review, echoed that it was "a painful test to write for a one-line fix."

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3609087: \Drupal\locale\LocaleProjectRepository::buildProjects() does not respect weight in 11.4.x
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +9/−0 · php +9
  • Diff: 94efcaf
  • Discussion: #3609087 · 3 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Sites that use hook_locale_translation_projects_alter() to set a weight key on translation projects will see that weight respected again when Drupal processes translation files. The weight was silently ignored in Drupal 11.4.x due to a regression, so any modules (such as locale_deploy) relying on this ordering mechanism would have had translation files processed in the wrong order.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The regression was introduced by the locale modernization refactor in #3037156. LocaleProjectRepository::buildProjects() constructs LocaleTranslatableProject objects using named arguments, but the weight named argument was never passed. As a result the weight set by hook_locale_translation_projects_alter() implementations was always discarded.

The fix is a one-liner: weight: $data['weight'] ?? 0 is added to the named-argument list in the LocaleTranslatableProject constructor call inside buildProjects(). A kernel test was added that sets the weight to 100 via a state flag (exercised through localeTranslationProjectsAlter() in the test module) and asserts getWeight() returns 100 after a fresh buildProjects() call. A baseline assertion confirming the default weight of 0 was also added.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 7, 2026
  • Committed: July 8, 2026

Key contributors

  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

Collaboration

nicxvan reviewed the patch, confirmed it was a straightforward fix, manually ran the tests, and verified the failure without the fix and the pass with it. This manual verification was notable because the test-only path could not be run against core committer pipelines.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3609020: BC layer in locale_translation_get_projects() implemented incorrectly
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +17/−0 · php +17
  • Diff: a009581
  • Discussion: #3609020 · 3 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

A regression in the backward-compatibility shim for locale_translation_get_projects() caused the function to return an empty result when called with no arguments on 11.4.x, where the correct behavior is to return all locale projects. Any code that still calls locale_translation_get_projects() without arguments was silently broken after upgrading to 11.4.x.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

When locale_translation_get_projects() was deprecated in 11.4.0 (see #3569330), the replacement shim always delegated to LocaleProjectRepository::getMultiple($project_names) regardless of whether $project_names was empty. The getMultiple() method interprets an empty array as "fetch nothing," but the original function's contract treats an empty array as "fetch all." The fix adds an empty($project_names) guard that routes to LocaleProjectRepository::getAll() when no project names are supplied, matching the pre-deprecation behavior. A new kernel test testLocaleTranslationGetProjects() in LocaleTranslationTest covers the no-argument, single-project, and nonexistent-project cases with the #[IgnoreDeprecations] attribute to suppress the expected deprecation notice during the test run.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 7, 2026
  • Committed: July 7, 2026

Key contributors

  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3605554: Translations are never loaded or downloaded for a custom profile
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +222/−27 · php +222/−27
  • Discussion: #3605554 · 5 contributors · 16 comments
  • Followers: 7

Summary

When running the Drupal installer with a custom install profile, any translatable strings introduced by that profile were never downloaded or loaded. Only Drupal core translations were fetched, leaving custom profile strings untranslated regardless of the selected language. This bug affected any project using a custom install profile, most visibly Drupal CMS, which required dubious workarounds to load its own translations. The fix makes the installer download translation files for the active profile (when it is non-core and has a known version) and makes the early-installer translation loader also read those profile-specific .po files.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Optional $project and $version parameters added to install_check_translations(); optional ?string $profile constructor parameter added to FileTranslation.

Technical details

install_download_translation() now calls install_check_translations() a second time for the active profile after downloading core translations. This second call is skipped when the profile path starts with core/ (i.e., it is a core-bundled profile) or when the profile has no declared version.

install_check_translations() previously hardcoded the project as 'drupal' and the version as \Drupal::VERSION. It now accepts two optional parameters, string $project = 'drupal' and string $version = \Drupal::VERSION, replacing those hardcoded values. This makes it reusable for any project whose translation files follow the standard localize.drupal.org naming convention.

FileTranslation::__construct() gains an optional ?string $profile parameter (constructor promotion). When a profile is provided, getTranslationFilesPattern() widens the filename regex from matching only drupal-<version>.<langcode>.po to matching (drupal|<profile>)-<version>.<langcode>.po. The version segment of the regex was also relaxed from a strict numeric pattern to .+ to accommodate the many version string formats used by contributed and custom profiles. install_begin_request() now passes $install_state['parameters']['profile'] to the FileTranslation service so it finds both sets of files during the early installer phase.

Upgrade

No changes required for existing callers: all new parameters are optional and default to their previous hardcoded values. If you added custom code to load profile translations as a workaround for this missing behavior (as Drupal CMS did), that code can now be removed.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 22, 2026

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

gábor hojtsy pointed out mid-review that issue #3594389 (removing the -dev suffix special-casing from locale project info) had just landed, which let phenaproxima simplify the implementation by dropping the -dev handling they had originally added. gábor also surfaced a nearly identical duplicate, #3529211, which phenaproxima had worked on a year earlier but which never landed.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3564112: Cache rebuild triggers file_get_contents Permission denied in theme node_modules despite file_scan_ignore_directories
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 10 files, +118/−10 · php +108/−10 · yml +10
  • Diff: 7e0c9a2
  • Discussion: #3564112 · 7 contributors · 41 comments
  • Followers: 8

Summary

Since Drupal 11.3 introduced OOP hook scanning for themes, cache rebuilds on sites with a node_modules/ directory inside a custom theme began emitting large numbers of file_get_contents() Permission denied warnings and could slow down dramatically. This happened because the new hook-collector classes did not respect the file_scan_ignore_directories setting. The fix makes HookCollectorPass and ThemeHookCollectorPass honour that setting, so directories listed there are skipped during hook discovery just as they already were during file and extension scanning.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Config change: The file_scan_ignore_directories setting now also controls which directories are skipped during hook-discovery scanning in HookCollectorPass and ThemeHookCollectorPass, not only file API and extension scanning.

Technical details

The 11.3 addition of ThemeHookCollectorPass and HookCollectorPass each contain a filterIterator() method that decides which directories to recurse into when scanning an extension for hook implementations. Those methods had a hardcoded exclusion list (['tests', 'js', 'css'] for modules; ['tests', 'js', 'css', 'templates'] for themes) but did not consult Settings::get('file_scan_ignore_directories'). As a result, node_modules/ trees inside theme directories were fully traversed, triggering StaticReflectionParser::parse() to attempt file_get_contents() on every file it encountered, many of which were unreadable directories on the developer's system.

The fix adds Settings::get('file_scan_ignore_directories', []) inside filterIterator() in both HookCollectorPass and ThemeHookCollectorPass, merging the result into the hardcoded exclusion array. The comment block for $settings['file_scan_ignore_directories'] in default.settings.php was also updated to document that the setting now governs hook-discovery scanning in addition to the file API and extension discovery. Tests covering both the module-hook and theme-hook cases were added under core/modules/system/tests/modules/HookCollector/ and core/modules/system/tests/themes/HookCollector/.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 19, 2025
  • Committed: July 6, 2026 (6 months and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • dotoree
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • richard.thomas
  • godotislate

Collaboration

The thread had a brief but substantive design debate. berdir's initial read-through suggested the fix should happen higher in the call stack and should respect the scan setting rather than only hardcoding directory names. nicxvan later floated the idea of simply hardcoding node_modules and bower_components rather than reusing file_scan_ignore_directories, reasoning the two concerns were distinct. richard.thomas pushed back by quoting the existing default.settings.php documentation, which already describes the setting as preventing "recursive scanning of directories looking for extensions," arguing the intent was identical. nicxvan agreed, and the approach of reading the setting at runtime was kept. The default.settings.php comment was then revised to make the broadened scope explicit.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3608374: Data for non-revisionable/non-translatable/no-dedicated-field-tables entity types such as file is loaded twice
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +11/−12 · php +11/−12
  • Diff: 978748d
  • Discussion: #3608374 · 3 contributors · 20 comments
  • Followers: 3

Summary

When loading entity types such as file that have no revision table, no data table, and no dedicated field tables, Drupal was issuing a redundant SQL query against the base table on every entity load. The extra query produced identical data to one already fired by getFromStorage(), so nothing useful came from it. This fix eliminates that query, cutting database load for content pages that reference files. Performance tests for the Umami demo profile show warm-cache query counts dropping by 5 queries per page request.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

In SqlContentEntityStorage::loadFromDedicatedTables(), the method builds a list of field "chunks" to fetch from dedicated and shared tables. When the count of single-cardinality fields does not exceed FIELD_MINIMUM_CHUNK_SIZE, the old else branch unconditionally set $chunks = [$single_cardinality_fields], even when $single_cardinality_fields was empty and the entity type had no $this->dataTable, $this->revisionTable, or $this->revisionDataTable. This produced a $chunks value of [0 => []], which caused the foreach loop to execute once with an empty fields list. That in turn triggered a SELECT * against the base table (e.g. file_managed) that duplicated the query already issued by buildQuery() in getFromStorage().

The fix initializes $chunks = [] before the conditional, then changes the else to an elseif that only creates the single-element chunk array when there is actually something to load: non-empty $single_cardinality_fields, or at least one of $this->dataTable, $this->revisionTable, or $this->revisionDataTable. With none of those present, the loop is skipped entirely and the redundant base-table query is never issued.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 4, 2026
  • Committed: July 6, 2026 (2 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • godotislate

Collaboration

berdir discovered the double query while profiling performance tests and noted the fix eliminates 12 to 14 queries on cold and cool caches in their own test setup. catch observed that the saving approaches a 10% query reduction on the lighter pages in the Umami performance suite.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3588547: Don't invalidate the entire fast backend on persistent backend cache misses
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +73/−12 · php +73/−12
  • Diff: 4f3e6a2
  • Discussion: #3588547 · 4 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 7

Summary

Sites using ChainedFastBackend (typically APCu as the fast layer in front of a database or Redis persistent layer) will see fewer unnecessary APCu invalidations when cache items are written. Previously, every set() or setMultiple() call caused the entire fast backend to be marked outdated, forcing subsequent reads to fall back to the persistent backend even when those reads had nothing to do with the items just written. Now, the invalidation only happens when the item being set already existed in the persistent backend, i.e. when it is truly being updated rather than freshly added. During cold cache warmup, where many new items are written in rapid succession, this eliminates a cascade of full-bin invalidations that was making the fast backend effectively useless until warming completed.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The key mechanism in ChainedFastBackend is a "last write timestamp" stored in the consistent backend. When markAsOutdated() is called, it bumps that timestamp, causing getMultiple() to treat all APCu hits as stale if they were written before the new timestamp. This timestamp approach is intentionally coarse: it invalidates the whole fast bin rather than individual entries.

Previously, set() and setMultiple() always called markAsOutdated() unconditionally after writing to the consistent backend. The fix changes both methods to first call consistentBackend->get() (or getMultiple() for the plural form) to check whether the item already exists. If the item is found, markAsOutdated() is called as before because existing fast-backend copies of that item are now stale. If the item is not found, no invalidation is needed: the fast backend cannot hold a stale copy of something it has never seen. The comment in the diff acknowledges a narrow race window where another process might write the same key between the get() check and the set(), but notes that such a process would also have received an empty get() result and would therefore not trigger invalidation either, keeping the risk of divergent state extremely low.

Unit tests in ChainedFastBackendTest were split from a single testSet() into testSetEmptyConsistent() (verifying that four writes to new keys produce no markAsOutdated() call and exactly four get() checks on the consistent backend) and testSetFullConsistent() (verifying that writes to pre-existing keys still trigger invalidation).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 5, 2026
  • Committed: July 6, 2026 (2 months and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • godotislate

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3607938: Chunking of Op in loadFromDedicatedTables causes Cartesian multiplication
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 5 files, +29/−27 · php +29/−27
  • Diff: c6b4102
  • Discussion: #3607938 · 8 contributors · 24 comments
  • Followers: 10

Summary

A critical regression introduced in Drupal 11.4 caused entity loading to generate catastrophically large SQL queries when an entity type had more than one unlimited-cardinality (dedicated table) field. Loading a single entity with several well-populated multi-value fields could produce millions of result rows via Cartesian multiplication, exhausting PHP memory and causing 502 errors and high CPU load regardless of how high the memory limit was set. This fix eliminates the Cartesian explosion by loading each multi-cardinality field in its own separate query instead of grouping them together.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The root cause was a chunking optimization in SqlContentEntityStorage::loadFromDedicatedTables(), introduced alongside the related 61-table JOIN limit fix (#3593963). When an entity had more than FIELD_MINIMUM_CHUNK_SIZE unlimited-cardinality fields, those fields were grouped into chunks and each chunk was passed to loadMultipleCardinalityFields() together. That method builds a single SELECT by joining each field's dedicated table to the base entity table. Joining N multi-value field tables in one query produces a Cartesian product: rows from field_a (5 values) × field_b (10 values) × field_c (4 values) = 200 rows instead of 19. On real content the multiplication was far worse; one confirmed case produced ~19.5 million rows per language per entity load.

The fix removes the chunking loop entirely. loadFromDedicatedTables() now iterates over $multiple_cardinality_fields one field at a time, calling loadMultipleCardinalityFields() with a single-element array on each iteration. This trades fewer queries for a guaranteed linear row count. Simultaneously, the join type inside loadMultipleCardinalityFields() was changed from leftJoin to join (INNER JOIN) for both the translatable and non-translatable code paths. A LEFT JOIN was unnecessary here because an entity that has no rows for a given dedicated field simply produces no result in that field's individual query; an INNER JOIN is correct and slightly more efficient.

The performance test fixtures reflect the new query shape: what was previously one multi-field LEFT OUTER JOIN query becomes N separate INNER JOIN queries, one per unlimited-cardinality field. Query counts in the Umami and standard performance tests increased accordingly (e.g., warm-cache node page rose from 56 to 65 queries). A follow-up issue #3608184 was opened to explore loading multiple fields without Cartesian multiplication (e.g., via UNION), which could recover some of that query count increase.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 2, 2026
  • Committed: July 3, 2026 (1 day and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • jviitamaki
  • thefancywizard
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • kulturmensch
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • godotislate

Collaboration

Several contributors quickly confirmed the severity before the fix was merged. thefancywizard reported that a node with 8 populated unlimited-cardinality fields generated a 14×13×11×8×7×6×3×2 = 4 million row query that took 134 seconds instead of ~235 ms, causing 502 errors and 800% CPU on their search pages, blocking an 11.4 production deployment entirely. kulturmensch tested MR !16200 against a real bilingual production site where one node had a theoretical Cartesian product of ~19.6 million rows per language; after the fix that node loaded in 0.56 seconds at 55 MB peak memory, and full sitemap generation of over 1,000 items completed successfully in under 30 seconds.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3608069: Restore (but don't apply) deprecated recipes to 11.4.x
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 25 files, +721/−0 · yml +641 · php +80
  • Diff: bae80c7
  • Discussion: #3608069 · 5 contributors · 12 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Five core recipes that were removed in Drupal 11.4.0 as part of the Standard profile content type cleanup (#3587118) have been restored to the 11.x and 11.4.x codebases. The removed recipes — article_content_type, page_content_type, article_comment, article_tags, and content_search — were still required as dependencies by third-party recipes and site templates (such as Commerce Kickstart) that build on the standard Article and Basic page content types. Their absence caused fatal errors when those downstream recipes tried to apply. The recipes are restored but no longer applied by the Standard profile or recipe. They will not be present in Drupal 12.x, giving the ecosystem one major version to migrate away from depending on them.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Five core recipes (article_content_type, page_content_type, article_comment, article_tags, content_search) are restored in 11.x/11.4.x but will not be present in Drupal 12.x; recipes depending on them must migrate before upgrading.

Technical details

The recipes were initially removed as part of #3587118, which decoupled the Standard profile from the Article and Basic page content types. That removal broke any recipe or site template that listed one of these five recipes in its own recipes: dependency key, producing a fatal error at apply time because the recipe directory no longer existed in core.

The fix is a direct partial revert: all five recipe directories are re-added under core/recipes/ on both the 11.x and 11.4.x branches, each containing its recipe.yml, the associated config files (node type definitions, field storages, field instances, entity form/view displays), and a GenericTest.php functional test that extends GenericRecipeTestBase. The 11.4.x and 11.x commits are identical in content. The recipes are deliberately absent from the 12.x branch.

A comment in the thread from phenaproxima articulates the backwards-compatibility principle at play: removing a recipe from a minor release is equivalent to removing a module from core in a minor release. A follow-up issue to explore formally marking recipes as deprecated was mentioned by gábor hojtsy and catch but is out of scope here.

Upgrade

If your custom recipe lists any of these in its recipes: key, it will continue to work on 11.x and 11.4.x. Plan to remove that dependency before upgrading to Drupal 12, where the recipes will not exist. For example, if your recipe.yml currently declares:

recipes:
  - article_content_type
  - page_content_type

You will need to either inline the equivalent config actions directly into your recipe, or require and ship those content type recipes yourself, before Drupal 12.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 2, 2026
  • Committed: July 3, 2026

Key contributors

  • rszrama (Centarro)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

phenaproxima's comment provided the key framing that swayed the thread quickly: removing these recipes in a minor release is equivalent to pulling a module out of core in a minor release, even if it cannot break live sites directly. That analogy established the backwards-compatibility obligation and made the path forward (restore in 11.x, remove in 12.x) immediately obvious to maintainers catch and longwave, who approved and committed within hours of the issue being filed.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3600697: Attribute route discovery does not handle arrays for JSON:API contrib modules
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +46/−24 · php +46/−24
  • Diff: 97a0dbb
  • Discussion: #3600697 · 6 contributors · 25 comments
  • Followers: 7

Summary

Sites using the JSON:API Resources or JSON:API Extras contrib modules were logging [warning] Array to string conversion AttributeRouteDiscovery.php:41 on every cache rebuild. This caused functional tests with failOnWarning=true to fail unexpectedly. The warning is now gone and attribute-based route discovery works correctly alongside those modules.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

AttributeRouteDiscovery::collectRoutes() (introduced in #3311365 to support PHP #[Route] attributes on controllers) iterated over container.namespaces and assumed every namespace value was a plain string directory path. It appended /Controller directly to that value. PSR-4 namespace registrations are allowed to map a single namespace to an array of base directories, and both JSON:API Resources and JSON:API Extras do exactly that: they register extra paths under the Drupal\jsonapi\Normalizer\ImpostorFrom\* namespace by pushing onto an existing container.namespaces key, producing an array. Concatenating a string onto an array triggered PHP's Array to string conversion notice and effectively made the scanner check a nonsense path, silently skipping attribute route discovery for those namespaces.

The fix wraps the per-namespace value in (array) and adds a middle loop, so a string value becomes a one-element array and an existing array is iterated as-is. The @param docblock on the constructor was updated to document the value type as string|array<int, string>. A new test covers the array-of-directories case using router_test_directory, including a deliberately missing path to verify the scanner skips non-existent directories gracefully.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 13, 2026
  • Committed: July 3, 2026 (19 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • ptmkenny
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • kieran.cott (Reading Room)
  • guillaumeg

Collaboration

kieran.cott diagnosed the bug clearly in comment #9: DrupalKernel::classLoaderAddMultiplePsr4() already documents and handles both string and array namespace values, so AttributeRouteDiscovery needed to match that contract. They opened an MR against 11.4.x. longwave noted there was also a merge conflict on main due to a parallel change (#3584793) and asked for a separate MR against main, which kieran.cott provided by reopening the earlier MR and rebasing it.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3594199: Remove the Telephone module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 17 files, +2/−645 · txt +1/−4 · php +1/−586 · yml +0/−43 · css +0/−12
  • Diff: 4cc588f
  • Discussion: #3594199 · 6 contributors · 24 comments
  • Followers: 7

Summary

The Telephone module has been removed from Drupal core. It provided a telephone field type, a telephone_link formatter, and a telephone_default widget for storing and displaying phone numbers. Sites that have telephone fields in use must uninstall the core module and switch to the community-maintained contrib version before or after upgrading. A change record with migration guidance is available at https://www.drupal.org/node/3606383.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: TelephoneItem, TelephoneLinkFormatter, and TelephoneDefaultWidget are removed from core; the telephone field type and its plugins no longer exist in Drupal\telephone\*.

Technical details

The entire core/modules/telephone/ directory was deleted, including TelephoneItem (field type), TelephoneLinkFormatter (formatter), TelephoneDefaultWidget (widget), TelephoneHooks (hook implementations), CSS icon assets, config schema (telephone.schema.yml), and the drupal.telephone-icon library. All associated tests were also removed.

telephone was added to the DRUPAL_CORE_REMOVED_MODULE_LIST constant in core/modules/system/src/Install/Requirements/SystemRequirements.php. This constant is checked during requirements verification, so any site with the telephone module still enabled will receive a blocking error when trying to run updates. The telephone namespace was also removed from the PHPStan suppression regex in core/phpstan-baseline.neon, and the telephone/drupal.telephone-icon library reference was removed from Stable9LibraryOverrideTest.

A follow-up issue (#3607231) was opened to regenerate the core database fixtures so that update path tests no longer need to carry a separate fixture to uninstall telephone before running.

Upgrade

Sites using the telephone field type must uninstall the core module before upgrading:

  1. Install the contrib replacement (https://www.drupal.org/node/3223395#s-telephone).
  2. Uninstall the core telephone module via Drush or the Extend UI.
  3. Update Drupal core.

If the module is still enabled at update time, SystemRequirements will block the update with an error because telephone is now listed in DRUPAL_CORE_REMOVED_MODULE_LIST.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 8, 2026
  • Committed: July 2, 2026 (24 days later)

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • dcam
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3605551: Allow install profiles to be automatically uninstalled
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 7 files, +112/−2 · php +94/−2 · yml +18
  • Diff: 3992c65
  • Discussion: #3605551 · 4 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Install profiles can now declare keep_profile: false in their .info.yml file to be automatically uninstalled at the end of the site installation process. Previously, a profile that existed only to customize the installer had no clean built-in way to remove itself: either the profile had to include explicit post-install uninstall code, or the site builder had to know to run drush pmu profile_name manually. With this change, setting keep_profile: false in the profile's info file causes Drupal's install_finished task to call module_installer->uninstall() on the profile automatically. The default behavior (profile remains installed) is unchanged when the key is absent or set to true.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New keep_profile key in install profile .info.yml files; when set to false the profile is automatically uninstalled at the end of install_finished.

Technical details

The change is a small addition to the install_finished() function in the install API. It reads $install_state['profile_info']['keep_profile'] (defaulting to true when absent). When the value is true, the existing module_set_weight($profile, 1000) call runs as before. When it is false, \Drupal::service('module_installer')->uninstall([$profile]) is called instead.

If the profile declares keep_profile: false but also installs modules that are depended on by other installed code, the standard module uninstall checks apply and throw an exception, aborting the install. This is tested by ProfileAutoUninstallFailureTest, which uses a fixture profile (testing_auto_uninstall_has_dependencies) that lists testing_in_use_dependency under install:. The success path is covered by ProfileAutoUninstallTest using the testing_auto_uninstall fixture profile, which verifies the profile key is absent from core.extension after install completes.

The naming follows the existing keep_english pattern already used in core profile info files.

Upgrade

Profile authors who want their profile to self-uninstall after site setup add one line to the profile's .info.yml:

keep_profile: false

No code changes are needed. The profile must not provide any modules that other installed extensions depend on, or the install will fail with an exception.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 22, 2026
  • Committed: July 2, 2026 (9 days and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3569137: Ensure TRUNCATE ::execute() method returns void
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +24/−27 · php +24/−27
  • Diff: 9eb69cb
  • Discussion: #3569137 · 7 contributors · 27 comments
  • Followers: 8

Summary

Drupal's database Truncate query class now officially returns nothing from its execute() method. Previously, Truncate::execute() nominally returned int|null (a row count or NULL), but because TRUNCATE is a DDL statement the value was unreliable and database-dependent. The return value has been dropped and the return type is now void. A new QueryExecutionVoidInterface interface has been introduced to formally express this contract. Developers whose code checked the return value of a truncate query need to remove that usage.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: Truncate::execute() return type changed from int|null to void; the returned row count is no longer available.
  • New API: New QueryExecutionVoidInterface declares public function execute(): void for DDL/DML queries that produce no return value.

Technical details

The fix has three parts. First, a new QueryExecutionVoidInterface (in core/lib/Drupal/Core/Database/Query/QueryExecutionVoidInterface.php) declares public function execute(): void for queries whose execution produces no meaningful return value. Second, Drupal\Core\Database\Query\Truncate now implements this interface: the execute() method gains a native void return type, the call to $statement->rowCount() is removed, and the fallback return NULL is removed. Third, the PostgreSQL-specific Drupal\Driver\Database\pgsql\Truncate override is updated the same way: it no longer captures or returns the result of parent::execute(). As a side effect, the abstract protected function execute() declaration was also removed from the base Query class, where it had been documented as returning StatementInterface|null; that abstract contract is no longer present.

Upgrade

Remove any code that reads the return value of Truncate::execute():

// Before
$count = $connection->truncate('my_table')->execute();
if ($count !== NULL) { ... } // unreliable; don't do this

// After
$connection->truncate('my_table')->execute();
// No return value; treat as void

If you type-hint against query objects that should not return a value, you may now use QueryExecutionVoidInterface.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 23, 2026
  • Committed: July 2, 2026 (5 months and 2 commits later)

Collaboration

The initial approach proposed in the MR introduced a new execution method alongside execute(), which drew pushback from catch (#6) who questioned why the existing method's return type couldn't simply be corrected. mondrake explained (#7) that PHP does not allow void in a union type, so narrowing the return type on execute() in the base Query class would be impossible while other implementations still return values. After alexpott (#13) revisited catch's concern and suggested leaving the API docs as-is and returning NULL, mondrake proposed (#14) introducing two distinct interfaces instead. The final MR applied that narrower interface approach only to Truncate, which catch then accepted and committed.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3584793: Use PHP attributes for form route discovery
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 9 files, +433/−84 · php +432/−75 · txt +1/−1 · yml +0/−8
  • Diff: 5bc84e8
  • Discussion: #3584793 · 3 contributors · 35 comments
  • Followers: 10

Summary

Module developers can now declare form routes using a #[Route] PHP attribute placed directly on a form class, instead of writing a corresponding entry in a *.routing.yml file. This extends the attribute-based route discovery introduced for controllers in #3311365 to cover Drupal forms as well. The same standard Symfony Route attribute is reused; no separate attribute class is needed. SiteInformationForm in the system module was converted as the first real-world example.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Module developers can place #[Route] directly on FormInterface classes in the Form namespace to declare routes, replacing equivalent YAML entries in *.routing.yml.

Technical details

AttributeRouteDiscovery::collectRoutes() now iterates over both Controller and Form subdirectories for every registered namespace. Files containing #[Route] in the Form tree are handled by a new createFormRouteCollection() method rather than the existing createControllerRouteCollection(). The form path checks that the reflected class implements FormInterface; classes in the Form namespace that do not implement it are silently skipped. When the interface is implemented, the route default _form is set to the fully-qualified class name (matching the YAML convention) and the class-level attribute is registered as a route. Route attributes on individual methods of a form class are explicitly unsupported: an \AssertionError is thrown in assert mode with a descriptive message. When exactly one route is registered for a form class, the class name itself is added as a route alias.

A new getReflectionClass() helper was extracted to centralize class existence and abstraction checks; it returns NULL and swallows \Error when a class cannot be loaded due to a missing parent, interface, or trait. A pre-existing bug in the invokable-controller path was also fixed: the condition $class->hasMethod('__invoke') === 0 (a boolean compared to an integer, always FALSE) was corrected to $collection->count() === 0 && $class->hasMethod('__invoke'). configureRoute() was removed; the _controller default is now set directly on the attribute object before addRoute() is called. The addRoute() $method parameter is now nullable to accommodate form routes, which are class-level rather than method-level.

Upgrade

To migrate an existing YAML form route to an attribute, remove the YAML entry and add #[Route] to the form class. Example from SiteInformationForm:

Before (system.routing.yml):

system.site_information_settings:
  path: '/admin/config/system/site-information'
  defaults:
    _form: '\Drupal\system\Form\SiteInformationForm'
    _title: 'Basic site settings'
  requirements:
    _permission: 'administer site configuration'

After (SiteInformationForm.php):

use Drupal\Core\StringTranslation\TranslatableMarkup;
use Symfony\Component\Routing\Attribute\Route;

#[Route(
  path: '/admin/config/system/site-information',
  name: 'system.site_information_settings',
  requirements: [
    '_permission' => 'administer site configuration',
  ],
  defaults: ['_title' => new TranslatableMarkup('Basic site settings')],
)]
class SiteInformationForm extends ConfigFormBase { ... }

The form class must implement FormInterface and must reside in a Form subdirectory/namespace of a registered module namespace. Route attributes on methods inside the form class are not supported and will throw an \AssertionError.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 14, 2026
  • Committed: July 1, 2026 (2 months and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • niklan

Collaboration

godotislate initially proposed a dedicated FormRoute attribute that extended the Symfony Route attribute, to keep form and controller discovery separate. After longwave suggested reusing the plain #[Route] attribute and detecting form classes internally via FormInterface, godotislate agreed immediately, dropped FormRoute, and rebuilt the implementation around FormInterface detection. That exchange directly shaped the final DX of the feature.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3591974: Update import paths for CKEditor 5 v48
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 36 files, +42/−51 · js +42/−51
  • Diff: fd41a57
  • Discussion: #3591974 · 5 contributors · 17 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Following the CKEditor 5 v48 upgrade in #3587182, the old sub-path import style for CKEditor 5 (such as ckeditor5/src/core) was sunset by the library. This issue updates all of Drupal core's built-in CKEditor 5 plugin source files to use the new unified ckeditor5 package import and rebuilds the compiled bundles, keeping the editor fully functional on Drupal 12 (main). This change does not affect Drupal 10 or 11.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

CKEditor 5's new installation method requires importing from the single unified ckeditor5 package rather than from deep sub-paths like ckeditor5/src/core. The compiled bundles for the five core CKEditor 5 plugins, drupalEmphasis, drupalHtmlEngine, drupalImage, drupalMedia, and drupalEntityLinkSuggestions, all had their webpack external binding updated from "ckeditor5/src/core" to "ckeditor5" in the minified output, reflecting matching source-file changes that were rebuilt with webpack.

During review, the question arose whether to also remove the now-dead externals patterns in webpack.config.js (the request.startsWith('ckeditor5/') and request.startsWith('@ckeditor/') branches that previously mapped old sub-path imports to the CKEDITOR UMD global). Testing confirmed these branches are dead code after the migration, but the cleanup was intentionally deferred to a follow-up to keep this patch focused.

Upgrade

Contrib or custom module developers who maintain CKEditor 5 plugins for Drupal 12 and use the old sub-path import style must update their source files:

// Before (old installation method, sunset in CKEditor 5 v48)
import { Plugin } from 'ckeditor5/src/core';
import { ButtonView } from 'ckeditor5/src/ui';

// After (new installation method)
import { Plugin, ButtonView } from 'ckeditor5';

After updating the source files, rebuild the plugin bundles with yarn build (or the equivalent). See the CKEditor 5 migration guide for full details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 26, 2026
  • Committed: June 30, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • pameeela (Technocrat)
  • godotislate
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • amitgoyal (Acquia)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3387100: Missing config schema for core.base_field_override.*.*.* third_party_settings.content_translation
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +32/−1 · php +25 · yml +7/−1
  • Diff: 22c7a86
  • Discussion: #3387100 · 12 contributors · 39 comments
  • Followers: 15

Summary

When enabling content translation on entity types such as media, Drupal writes core.base_field_override.*.*.* config entities that may carry a third_party_settings.content_translation entry. No config schema existed for that key, causing config inspector and strict schema validation to report errors. The fix adds the missing schema and includes a post-update hook that resaves all affected base field override entities so that previously stored values (for example integer 0 instead of string '0') are normalized to match the new schema.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Database update: New post-update hook content_translation_post_update_resave_base_field_overrides resaves base field override config entities that have content translation third-party settings to normalize stored values against the new schema.

Technical details

The schema file content_translation.schema.yml previously defined field.field.*.*.*.third_party.content_translation inline. The fix extracts that mapping into a reusable named type content_translation_field_third_party_settings and references it from both the existing field.field key and the newly added core.base_field_override.*.*.*.third_party.content_translation key.

A new content_translation_post_update_resave_base_field_overrides() function in content_translation.post_update.php iterates all base_field_override entities via ConfigEntityUpdater and resaves only those that have content_translation third-party settings. Resaving forces Drupal to cast stored values through the schema, converting any integer 0 / 1 values to the string type the schema requires. A test assertion was also added to ContentLanguageBaseFieldSyncWidgetTest::testBaseFieldSyncOptionsVisibleOnFirstLoad() verifying that saving the content-language settings form produces no config schema errors.

Upgrade

Run database updates after deploying this change so that existing base field override config entities with content translation settings are resaved and normalized:

drush updatedb
# or
php core/scripts/drupal.php update

No code changes are required in custom modules.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 13, 2023
  • Committed: June 30, 2026 (2 years and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • grimreaper (Smile)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • heddn (MTech, LLC)
  • bbrala (SWIS)
  • bbu23 (Nazaries Intelligenia)
  • codebymikey (Zodiac Media)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

The thread ran for nearly two years before landing. A long-standing question was whether an update hook was needed or would be too disruptive. Contributor heddn (comment #12) argued that a hook would force unwanted config exports for sites that never exported their base field overrides. Contributor bbu23 later demonstrated concretely (comment #18) that without resaving, integer 0 values stored by older Drupal versions still trigger a type-mismatch error even with the new schema present. Contributor codebymikey then added the post-update hook targeting only base field overrides that already carry content_translation third-party settings, limiting the resave footprint. Maintainer alexpott accelerated the final review after the related issue #3606969 caused contrib test failures, and longwave committed the result.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3600644: Pull up attributes from block plugins if the render array has no type or theme on the top level
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +37/−0 · php +37
  • Diff: ec24cbd
  • Discussion: #3600644 · 5 contributors · 19 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

Block plugins that return a plain render array (one with no #type or #theme at the top level) with #attributes set will again have those attributes hoisted onto the block wrapper element, restoring behavior that worked correctly up to Drupal 11.3. In 11.4 those attributes were silently dropped, breaking a common workaround pattern used by module developers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The regression was introduced by #2486267, which reworked how BlockViewBuilder::preRender() handles attributes. That change only merged attributes when the plugin's content array carried #wrapper_attributes; any other top-level #attributes were simply discarded.

The fix adds an elseif branch in BlockViewBuilder::preRender() immediately after the #wrapper_attributes path: when #wrapper_attributes is absent and the content render element has neither #type nor #theme, any #attributes on the content are hoisted into $build['#attributes'] and removed from the child array. This covers the typical "plain build array with custom attributes" pattern that many contrib and custom module block plugins rely on. A kernel test using the existing test_html block plugin was added to verify the hoisting behavior and confirm the fix against the regression. A follow-up issue (#3605585) tracks whether this compatibility path should eventually be deprecated.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 13, 2026
  • Committed: June 29, 2026 (16 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • amitgoyal (Acquia)
  • godotislate
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

Collaboration

berdir reported and authored the fix, noting the pattern was a common workaround and proposing that BC restoration should be conditional on the absence of #type and #theme. godotislate (after consulting with catch) confirmed it should land in 11.4, and raised the question of deprecation messaging. berdir argued to commit the hotfix first and handle deprecation in a follow-up, which both catch and godotislate agreed with. berdir subsequently opened #3605585 for that discussion.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3600889: Remove some minor constraints from core-recommended
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +19/−16 · php +18/−3 · json +1/−13
  • Diff: 8db15f2
  • Discussion: #3600889 · 21 contributors · 63 comments
  • Followers: 54

Summary

Sites using drupal/core-recommended were regularly blocked from running composer update whenever Guzzle, Symfony polyfills, or Twig shipped a security fix in a new minor version, because core-recommended pinned those packages to a specific minor. The fix removes the minor-version pins for guzzlehttp/guzzle, guzzlehttp/promises, guzzlehttp/psr7, all symfony/polyfill-* packages, twig/twig, and twig/html-extra from the generated metapackage. Sites can now install security updates for these libraries as soon as they are available, without needing a new Drupal core release. Related security pressure came from issues like #3599842 and #3592065. A follow-up (#3606820) tracks adding a separate, more-frequent release process for core-recommended so that future constraint adjustments can ship independently of core.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The drupal/core-recommended metapackage is generated from composer/Plugin/Metapackage/CoreRecommended/src/Builder.php. That class maintains a $remove_list: any package in this list is omitted from the pinned require section of the generated composer/Metapackage/CoreRecommended/composer.json, meaning Composer will fall back to the broader constraints in drupal/core's own composer.json rather than the tighter minor-version pins (~x.y.z) that core-recommended had previously added.

The commit adds guzzlehttp/guzzle, guzzlehttp/promises, guzzlehttp/psr7, symfony/polyfill-ctype, symfony/polyfill-iconv, symfony/polyfill-intl-grapheme, symfony/polyfill-intl-idn, symfony/polyfill-intl-normalizer, symfony/polyfill-mbstring, symfony/polyfill-php86, twig/html-extra, and twig/twig to that remove list. Each removal is annotated with its rationale: Twig and Guzzle regularly issue security releases only as new minor versions, and the Symfony polyfills are transitive dependencies that follow the same pattern. The generated composer/Metapackage/CoreRecommended/composer.json was regenerated after these changes. The earlier approach of removing all minor constraints from the metapackage (MRs !16071, !16072, !16073) was set to draft in favour of this targeted removal; symfony/yaml and the full Symfony component stack retain their minor-version pins for now.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 15, 2026
  • Committed: June 29, 2026 (14 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • moshe weitzman
  • deviantintegral
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • joelpittet (The University of British Columbia)
  • godotislate
  • borisson_ (Calibrate)
  • ressa
  • xjm (Zoocha)
  • prudloff (Insite)
  • solideogloria
  • cmlara (DM13 Security LLC)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • steven jones (ComputerMinds)
  • jurgenhaas (LakeDrops)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • lambert
  • benjifisher (Harvard Web Publishing)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)

Collaboration

The thread went through a significant design shift. The initial proposal and first set of MRs removed all minor constraints from core-recommended entirely. Several contributors, including xjm and joelpittet, pushed back on this as too drastic and potentially confusing, questioning the purpose of the metapackage without any pinning. benjifisher suggested a middle ground: relax constraints only for the specific packages known to publish security-only minor releases, twig/twig, guzzlehttp/*, and symfony/polyfill-*. That targeted approach is what landed. longwave noted during final review that guzzlehttp/promises and the PHP 8.4/8.5 polyfills should also be included, and catch added those before the commit.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.
